title: ACC SRVY 2339 — GPS/GIS Applications in Surveying

lessons.title: Week 1: Introduction to Satellite Positioning and GNSS Overview
learning_objectives: Define GNSS and distinguish it from GPS
Identify the major global and regional satellite constellations
Explain the three segments of a satellite positioning system
Describe the basic principle of trilateration from satellite ranges
key_takeaways: GNSS is the umbrella term; GPS is the U.S. constellation within it
A receiver needs at least four satellites to solve for X, Y, Z, and the clock bias
Every system has space, control, and user segments
Positioning is trilateration from measured satellite-to-receiver ranges

content.html:
From GPS to GNSS

GNSS stands for Global Navigation Satellite System, the generic term for any constellation of satellites that broadcasts ranging signals a receiver can use to compute its position, velocity, and time (PVT). The U.S. GPS (Global Positioning System) is one such constellation, but it is no longer alone. Modern survey receivers are multi-constellation, tracking GPS together with Russia’s GLONASS, the European Union’s Galileo, and China’s BeiDou, plus regional systems. Tracking more constellations means more satellites in view, better geometry, faster initialization, and more reliable results under canopy or in urban canyons.

For a surveyor, the practical takeaway is that “GPS” in everyday speech usually means GNSS. The hardware, math, and field procedures are largely common across constellations; the receiver simply has more signals to choose from. Throughout this course we use GNSS as the general term and GPS when we mean the U.S. system specifically.

Three segments of every system

Each GNSS is organized into three parts. The space segment is the constellation of satellites in medium Earth orbit (about 20,200 km for GPS) that broadcast ranging codes and navigation messages. The control segment is the network of ground monitoring and uplink stations that track the satellites, compute precise orbits and clock corrections, and upload updated navigation data. The user segment is everyone with a receiver — surveyors, machine-control systems, phones, ships, and aircraft. A failure or degradation in the control segment (bad uploaded ephemeris, for instance) propagates directly into user positions, which is why surveyors verify data quality rather than trusting a fix blindly.

content.text: At least four satellites are required for a 3D fix: three to solve the position unknowns (X, Y, Z) and a fourth to solve the receiver clock bias. More satellites improve geometry and provide redundancy.

content.html:
How positioning works: trilateration

A receiver determines position by trilateration from ranges. Each satellite broadcasts a precisely timed signal; the receiver measures how long the signal took to arrive and multiplies by the speed of light to get a range. One range places the receiver somewhere on a sphere around that satellite; two ranges intersect to a circle; three ranges intersect to a point (in principle). Because the receiver’s own clock is cheap and inaccurate, the measured ranges are biased by a common clock error — they are called pseudoranges. The fourth satellite lets the receiver solve for that clock bias along with position, which is why four satellites are the practical minimum.

content.title: Key Takeaways
items: GNSS is the umbrella term; GPS, GLONASS, Galileo, and BeiDou are the four global constellations
Space, control, and user segments make up every system
Trilateration from at least four satellite ranges yields position and receiver clock bias
Multi-constellation receivers see more satellites and produce more robust fixes

flashcards.definition: Global Navigation Satellite System — the generic term for any satellite positioning constellation; GPS is the U.S. example.
A satellite-to-receiver range computed from signal travel time that is biased by the receiver clock error.
Space (satellites), control (ground tracking/upload stations), and user (receivers).

lessons.title: Week 2: GPS Signal Structure and Satellite Orbits
learning_objectives: Describe the GPS carrier frequencies L1, L2, and L5
Distinguish the C/A and P(Y) ranging codes and the navigation message
Explain code-phase vs. carrier-phase observables
Describe satellite orbits, ephemeris, and almanac data
key_takeaways: GPS broadcasts on carrier frequencies derived from a 10.23 MHz fundamental
Code-phase measurements are coarse; carrier-phase measurements are precise but ambiguous
The navigation message carries ephemeris (precise orbit) and almanac (coarse, all satellites)
Surveying accuracy comes from resolving the carrier-phase integer ambiguity

content.html:
The GPS signal

GPS satellites transmit on several carrier frequencies derived from a fundamental 10.23 MHz oscillator. The legacy carriers are L1 (1575.42 MHz) and L2 (1227.60 MHz); modernized satellites add L5 (1176.45 MHz). Onto these carriers the satellite modulates two things: ranging codes and a navigation message. The codes are pseudo-random noise (PRN) sequences unique to each satellite, which lets all satellites share the same frequencies (code-division multiple access). The navigation message tells the receiver where the satellite is and how its clock behaves.

Codes and the navigation message

Two ranging codes ride on the carriers. The C/A code (Coarse/Acquisition) is the open civilian code on L1; it is easy to acquire and gives meter-level code-phase ranging. The P(Y) code (Precise, encrypted) is the military code on L1 and L2. The navigation message superimposed on the codes contains the broadcast ephemeris (precise orbital parameters for that satellite, valid for a few hours), satellite clock corrections, health flags, and the almanac (coarse orbital data for the whole constellation, used for mission planning and acquisition).

content.text: Ephemeris = precise orbit for one satellite, short validity. Almanac = coarse orbit for all satellites, long validity. Mission-planning software uses the almanac to predict satellite visibility and geometry.

content.html:
Code-phase vs. carrier-phase

There are two ways to measure range. Code-phase ranging correlates the received PRN code with a receiver-generated replica; it is unambiguous but coarse (decimeter-to-meter precision), and is what handheld and navigation receivers use. Carrier-phase ranging measures the fraction of a carrier wavelength (about 19 cm on L1) and counts whole cycles; it is roughly 100× more precise but carries an unknown integer ambiguity — the number of whole wavelengths between satellite and receiver. Survey-grade positioning (static, RTK, PPK) achieves centimeter accuracy precisely because it resolves that integer ambiguity and uses the carrier phase.

content.label: Carrier-phase observable: measured fractional phase plus unknown integer N

content.title: Key Takeaways
items: L1, L2, and L5 carriers all derive from the 10.23 MHz fundamental frequency
C/A is the open civilian code; P(Y) is encrypted military
Ephemeris is the precise short-term orbit; almanac is the coarse constellation-wide orbit
Carrier-phase ranging is precise but ambiguous; resolving the integer ambiguity gives survey accuracy

flashcards.definition: Ephemeris is precise orbit data for one satellite valid a few hours; almanac is coarse orbit data for the whole constellation valid weeks.
The unknown whole number of carrier wavelengths between satellite and receiver that must be resolved for centimeter accuracy.
flashcards.hint_1: It is an integer count of full cycles

flashcards.definition: The open Coarse/Acquisition pseudo-random code on L1 used for civilian code-phase ranging.

lessons.title: Week 3: GPS Error Sources and Mitigation
learning_objectives: Identify the major GNSS error sources
Explain ionospheric and tropospheric delay and how each is mitigated
Define multipath and describe field mitigation
Compute position error from UERE and DOP
key_takeaways: Errors come from satellites, the atmosphere, and the receiver/site
Dual-frequency observations remove most of the ionospheric delay
Multipath is a site-dependent error reduced by antenna choice and siting
Position accuracy = UERE multiplied by the relevant DOP value

content.html:
Where the errors come from

Every pseudorange and carrier-phase measurement is corrupted by errors that can be grouped by source. Satellite errors include orbit (ephemeris) error and satellite clock error. Atmospheric errors are the ionospheric delay (a dispersive, frequency-dependent bending and slowing of the signal in the charged upper atmosphere) and the tropospheric delay (a non-dispersive wet/dry delay in the lower atmosphere). Receiver and site errors include receiver clock and noise, antenna phase-center variation, and multipath — signals reflecting off nearby surfaces before reaching the antenna. The combined ranging error per satellite is summarized as the UERE (User Equivalent Range Error).

Atmospheric delays

The ionosphere is dispersive: its delay depends on frequency, so a receiver tracking two frequencies (L1 and L2, or L1/L5) can form a linear combination that removes nearly all of the first-order ionospheric error. This is the single biggest advantage of dual-frequency surveying. The troposphere is non-dispersive (same delay at all GNSS frequencies), so it cannot be removed by combining frequencies; instead it is handled with models (e.g., Saastamoinen, Hopfield) and, in precise processing, estimated as a parameter. Both delays grow as satellites get lower in the sky, which is why a 10–15° elevation mask is standard.

content.text: Multipath cannot be differenced away — it is unique to each antenna and site. Mitigate it in the field: choose an open site, avoid reflective surfaces (buildings, water, vehicles, chain-link), use a ground plane or choke-ring antenna, and occupy long enough to average it down.

content.html:
UERE, DOP, and position accuracy

The expected accuracy of a position is the product of the ranging error and the satellite geometry. The UERE rolls all per-satellite range errors into one number (a standard deviation in meters). DOP (Dilution of Precision) is a unitless multiplier that captures how favorable the satellite geometry is — satellites well spread across the sky give a low (good) DOP, while clustered satellites give a high (poor) DOP. The expected position error is simply UERE × DOP. PDOP (position), HDOP (horizontal), VDOP (vertical), and GDOP (geometric, including time) describe different components.

content.label: Expected position error from range error and geometry

content.title: Key Takeaways
items: Errors group into satellite, atmospheric, and receiver/site categories
Dual-frequency removes the first-order ionospheric delay; the troposphere is modeled
Multipath is site-specific and mitigated by antenna and siting, not differencing
Position error = UERE times DOP; good geometry means low DOP

flashcards.definition: User Equivalent Range Error — the combined one-sigma ranging error per satellite, in meters.
A unitless multiplier describing satellite geometry; low DOP = good geometry. Position error = UERE x DOP.
Error caused by signals reflecting off nearby surfaces before reaching the antenna; reduced by siting and antenna design.

lessons.title: Week 4: Coordinate Systems, Datums, and Projections
learning_objectives: Distinguish geocentric, geographic, and projected coordinate systems
Explain reference ellipsoids and horizontal datums (NAD83, WGS84, ITRF)
Describe the State Plane Coordinate System and UTM
Explain the relationship between grid and ground distances
key_takeaways: GNSS works natively in geocentric ECEF / WGS84 coordinates
A datum is an ellipsoid plus its realization (control + epoch)
State Plane projects a state onto Lambert or transverse Mercator zones
Grid distances differ from ground distances by the combined scale factor

content.html:
Three families of coordinates

GNSS positions can be expressed three ways. Geocentric (ECEF) coordinates are Cartesian X, Y, Z with the origin at the Earth’s mass center — this is what the receiver actually solves. Geographic coordinates are latitude, longitude, and ellipsoidal height referenced to a reference ellipsoid. Projected (plane) coordinates are northing and easting on a flat map, produced by a map projection so surveyors can use plane trigonometry. Software converts among these continuously; the surveyor must know which system a coordinate is in before using it.

Datums and ellipsoids

A horizontal datum is a reference ellipsoid (a smooth mathematical Earth model with a defined semi-major axis and flattening) plus a realization — the network of control monuments and an epoch that ties the ellipsoid to the physical Earth. WGS84 is the GPS native datum; NAD83 is the North American datum used for U.S. surveying and mapping; ITRF is the global reference frame WGS84 is aligned to. NAD83 and WGS84 differ by 1–2 meters because of how each is defined and because tectonic plates move — this matters for any survey tying GNSS to published NAD83 control. NGS is replacing NAD83 with new reference frames (e.g., NATRF2022) in the modernized NSRS.

content.text: NAD83 and WGS84 are NOT the same datum. Treating GNSS WGS84 coordinates as NAD83 introduces a 1-2 m horizontal shift. Always know and document which datum, realization, and epoch your coordinates use.

content.html:
State Plane and UTM projections

To work in plane coordinates, surveyors project the ellipsoid onto a flat grid. The State Plane Coordinate System (SPCS) divides each state into zones, using a Lambert Conformal Conic projection for zones wider east-west and a Transverse Mercator for zones taller north-south, keeping distortion under about 1:10,000. Texas, for example, has five SPCS zones (North, North Central, Central, South Central, South). UTM divides the world into 6°-wide transverse Mercator zones. Because a projection cannot preserve true distance everywhere, grid distances differ from ground distances; the next lessons address heights and scale factors that reconcile them.

content.title: Key Takeaways
items: ECEF/geographic/projected are three views of the same position
A datum is an ellipsoid plus its control realization and epoch
WGS84 (GPS) and NAD83 (US surveying) differ by 1-2 m
State Plane uses Lambert or transverse Mercator zones to limit distortion

flashcards.definition: A reference ellipsoid plus its realization (control + epoch) that defines latitude and longitude.
A conformal plane-grid system dividing each state into zones (Lambert conic or transverse Mercator) with distortion held under ~1:10,000.
flashcards.hint_1: Texas has five of these zones

flashcards.definition: Earth-Centered, Earth-Fixed geocentric Cartesian X, Y, Z coordinates — the native frame of a GNSS solution.

lessons.title: Week 5: Geoid Models and Height Systems
learning_objectives: Distinguish ellipsoidal, orthometric, and geoid heights
Apply the relationship H = h - N
Explain geoid models (GEOID18) and their role in GNSS heighting
Recognize why GNSS gives ellipsoidal height, not elevation
key_takeaways: GNSS measures ellipsoidal height h, not orthometric elevation H
The geoid undulation N converts between them: H = h - N
Orthometric height is what plans, FEMA, and elevations refer to
A current geoid model (e.g., GEOID18) is required for accurate GNSS elevations

content.html:
Three kinds of height

Height is surprisingly subtle in GNSS surveying because three different surfaces are involved. Ellipsoidal height (h) is the distance from the reference ellipsoid up to the point, measured along the ellipsoid normal — this is what GNSS directly produces. Orthometric height (H) is the height above the geoid (mean sea level surface) measured along the plumb line — this is the “elevation” on plans, benchmarks, and flood maps. The geoid height or undulation (N) is the separation between the ellipsoid and the geoid at that point. In the conterminous U.S., N is negative (the geoid lies below the ellipsoid), ranging roughly from −8 m to −53 m.

content.label: Orthometric height from ellipsoidal height and geoid undulation

content.html:
Why this matters in the field

A GNSS receiver reports h, but a contractor, a benchmark, a FEMA flood elevation, and a topographic plan all use H. If you hand a contractor raw ellipsoidal heights, every elevation will be wrong by N — tens of meters. To get correct elevations you must apply a geoid model, which provides N at any latitude/longitude. NGS’s GEOID18 is the current hybrid geoid model for NAD83 in the U.S.; survey software interpolates N from its grid and computes H = h − N automatically when the correct model is loaded.

content.text: Never report GNSS ellipsoidal height as elevation. Always apply the correct, current geoid model so that elevations are orthometric (H = h - N). Mixing h and H is a classic, costly blunder on construction and flood work.

content.html:
Accuracy of GNSS heights

GNSS heights are inherently weaker than horizontal positions (VDOP exceeds HDOP because all satellites are above the horizon). Orthometric accuracy is also limited by the geoid model itself. For the best vertical results, surveyors use long static occupations, dual-frequency observations, the latest geoid model, and where elevations are critical they tie to published NAVD88 benchmarks by leveling rather than trusting GNSS heights alone.

content.title: Key Takeaways
items: GNSS gives ellipsoidal height h; plans use orthometric elevation H
H = h - N, where N is the geoid undulation from a geoid model
GEOID18 is the current NGS hybrid model for NAD83
GNSS vertical accuracy is weaker than horizontal; verify against benchmarks

flashcards.definition: The separation between the ellipsoid and the geoid at a point; used to convert h to H. Negative across the lower 48 states.
Orthometric height equals ellipsoidal height minus geoid undulation.
The current NGS hybrid geoid model that supplies N for converting GNSS ellipsoidal heights to NAVD88-style orthometric elevations.

lessons.title: Week 6: Static GPS Surveying
learning_objectives: Describe static and rapid-static GNSS surveying
Explain baselines, sessions, and occupation times
Describe relative positioning and double differencing conceptually
Identify when static methods are appropriate
key_takeaways: Static surveying is the most accurate relative GNSS technique
Two receivers observing simultaneously form a baseline vector
Longer baselines and weaker geometry require longer occupations
Static is used for control, geodetic ties, and high-accuracy networks

content.html:
What static surveying is

Static GNSS surveying is the classic, highest-accuracy relative positioning method. Two or more receivers occupy stations simultaneously and log raw carrier-phase data for an extended period (often 20 minutes to several hours). After the session, the data are post-processed to compute the baseline vector (the ΔX, ΔY, ΔZ) between each pair of receivers. Because errors common to both receivers (satellite clock, much of the orbit and atmosphere) cancel in the differencing, static surveying delivers the best precision available — millimeters plus a few parts per million of baseline length.

Baselines and differencing

A single receiver alone gives an absolute point position limited by all the error sources. Relative (differential) positioning instead computes the vector between two receivers. By forming double differences — differencing measurements between two satellites and between two receivers — the satellite clock error, receiver clock error, and most atmospheric error cancel, and the carrier-phase integer ambiguities become resolvable. The result is a precise baseline that can be adjusted into a network. This is why survey-grade work always uses at least two receivers (or one rover plus a base/CORS).

content.text: Occupation time should grow with baseline length and worsening geometry. A short baseline under open sky may need only minutes; a long baseline or obstructed site may need hours to resolve ambiguities reliably.

content.html:
Baseline error budget

Static baseline accuracy is usually quoted as a constant plus a length-dependent term: a few millimeters of fixed error plus a few parts per million (ppm) of the baseline length. For example, a 5 mm + 1 ppm specification on a 10 km baseline gives 5 mm + (1 ppm × 10,000 m) = 5 mm + 10 mm = 15 mm. The ppm term reflects errors (orbit, atmosphere) that scale with separation, which is another reason to keep baselines reasonably short and to tie to nearby CORS.

content.label: Baseline error: constant a (mm) plus b (ppm) times length D (km)

content.title: Key Takeaways
items: Static surveying logs long sessions and post-processes baselines for top accuracy
Two simultaneous receivers form a baseline vector
Double differencing cancels clock and most atmospheric errors
Baseline error = a (mm) + b (ppm) x distance; longer baselines need longer occupations

flashcards.definition: The 3D vector (delta X, Y, Z) between two simultaneously observing GNSS receivers.
Forming differences between two satellites and two receivers to cancel clock and atmospheric errors and enable ambiguity resolution.
A short-occupation static method (minutes) for short baselines and control densification.

lessons.title: Week 7: Real-Time Kinematic (RTK) GPS Surveying
learning_objectives: Explain the principle of RTK positioning
Describe the base-rover correction link
Distinguish fixed vs. float solutions and initialization
Identify RTK accuracy and limitations
key_takeaways: RTK delivers centimeter results in real time using a correction link
A base on known control streams corrections to a moving rover
A "fixed" solution means integer ambiguities are resolved; "float" is not yet reliable
RTK accuracy degrades with baseline length, multipath, and poor geometry

content.html:
Real-time kinematic positioning

RTK brings carrier-phase, centimeter-level accuracy into the field in real time. A base receiver sits on a known point and continuously computes corrections, which it transmits over a radio or cellular link to a rover. The rover applies the corrections to its own observations, resolves the carrier-phase ambiguities on the fly, and reports a centimeter-accurate position every second or faster. RTK is the workhorse for topographic mapping, construction stakeout, and as-builts because it is fast and the surveyor sees the result immediately.

Fixed vs. float

The single most important indicator on an RTK data collector is the solution status. A float solution means the integer ambiguities have not yet been resolved — the position may be off by decimeters and must not be trusted for survey work. A fixed solution means the integers are resolved and the position is good to a centimeter or two. The transition from float to fixed is called initialization. Surveyors wait for “fixed,” verify it on a known point, and re-initialize after any loss of lock (driving under trees, beside buildings).

content.text: Never record RTK points on a FLOAT solution. Only a FIXED (ambiguity-resolved) solution gives survey-grade accuracy. Always check into a known point at the start and periodically to confirm the fix is valid.

content.html:
RTK accuracy and limits

A fixed RTK solution is typically good to about 1–2 cm horizontally and 2–3 cm vertically, with a small ppm term that grows with the base-rover distance. Single-base RTK is generally reliable out to about 10–20 km before the correction loses validity (the atmosphere over base and rover diverges). RTK is degraded by multipath, overhead obstructions, weak radio/cell coverage, and poor geometry. For larger areas or longer ranges, surveyors move to Network RTK (next lesson), which models the atmosphere across a region of reference stations.

content.title: Key Takeaways
items: RTK gives real-time centimeter positions via a base-to-rover correction link
Only a FIXED solution is survey-grade; FLOAT is not
Initialization is the float-to-fixed transition; re-initialize after loss of lock
Single-base RTK is reliable to roughly 10-20 km

flashcards.definition: Real-Time Kinematic — carrier-phase positioning that yields centimeter accuracy in real time using base-to-rover corrections.
An RTK solution with integer ambiguities resolved, accurate to a centimeter or two. (Float = unresolved, not trustworthy.)
The process of resolving ambiguities so the solution transitions from float to fixed.

lessons.title: Week 8: Midterm Exam
learning_objectives: Review GNSS fundamentals, signals, errors, datums, heights, and methods
Self-assess readiness across the first half of the course
Apply core formulas (H = h - N, baseline error, UERE x DOP)
key_takeaways: The midterm covers Weeks 1-7
Review signal structure, error sources, datums, geoid heights, static, and RTK
Be able to apply the core computational relationships

content.html:
Midterm overview

The Week 8 midterm covers the first half of the course: GNSS fundamentals and constellations (Week 1), signal structure and orbits (Week 2), error sources and mitigation (Week 3), coordinate systems and datums (Week 4), geoid models and heights (Week 5), static surveying (Week 6), and RTK (Week 7). Expect a mix of conceptual multiple-choice questions and short computations.

What to be able to do

You should be able to: name the four global constellations and the three system segments; explain code-phase vs. carrier-phase and the integer ambiguity; list and mitigate the major error sources; convert between ellipsoidal and orthometric height with H = h − N; compute expected position error as UERE × DOP; estimate a static baseline error as a + b·D; and explain the difference between fixed and float RTK solutions and between WGS84 and NAD83.

content.text: Bring a calculator. Practice the four formula types: H = h - N; baseline a + b*D; UERE x PDOP; and grid/ground scale factor conversions.

content.title: Key Takeaways
items: The midterm spans Weeks 1-7
Master the four core formulas and the conceptual distinctions
Know fixed vs. float and WGS84 vs. NAD83 cold

flashcards.definition: Weeks 1-7: fundamentals, signals, errors, datums, heights, static, and RTK.
H = h - N; baseline error a + b*D; position error UERE x DOP; grid = ground x combined scale factor.

lessons.title: Week 9: Network RTK, VRS, and CORS
learning_objectives: Explain Network RTK and the VRS concept
Describe the CORS network and its purpose
Explain OPUS and post-processed positioning from CORS
Compare single-base RTK with Network RTK
key_takeaways: Network RTK models atmospheric error across a region of reference stations
A VRS synthesizes corrections for a virtual base near the rover
CORS are permanent reference stations providing data for OPUS and post-processing
OPUS returns NSRS coordinates from a submitted static file

content.html:
Beyond a single base

Single-base RTK weakens as the rover moves away from the base because the atmosphere over the two sites diverges. Network RTK solves this by using a network of permanent reference stations. A central server collects all their data, models the spatially-varying errors (ionosphere, troposphere, orbits) across the whole region, and generates corrections valid anywhere inside the network. The rover sends its approximate position to the server and receives tailored corrections — extending reliable centimeter RTK across tens of kilometers without a local base.

VRS and other network methods

The most common implementation is the Virtual Reference Station (VRS): the network synthesizes corrections as if a base existed right next to the rover (the “virtual” station), so the baseline is effectively near zero and the rover behaves like it has a base a few meters away. Other approaches include MAC and FKP. All require a two-way data link (cellular) so the rover can send its position and receive corrections. The surveyor subscribes to a network RTK service rather than setting up and occupying a physical base.

CORS and OPUS

The U.S. CORS (Continuously Operating Reference Stations) network, coordinated by NGS, is a set of permanent GNSS stations with precisely known NSRS coordinates that log data continuously and make it freely available. CORS underpin Network RTK and post-processing: a surveyor can collect a static file at an unknown point and process it against nearby CORS. NGS’s OPUS (Online Positioning User Service) automates this — you upload a dual-frequency static file, and OPUS returns NSRS coordinates (NAD83 and ITRF, with state plane and UTM) by processing against three CORS. OPUS-S works on 15 min–48 hr of data.

content.text: OPUS is free and authoritative: submit a quality dual-frequency static occupation (longer is better) and receive published-grade NSRS coordinates tied to the national network — ideal for establishing project control.

content.title: Key Takeaways
items: Network RTK models regional atmospheric error using many reference stations
VRS synthesizes a virtual base next to the rover
CORS are permanent NGS stations supplying free continuous data
OPUS post-processes a submitted static file into NSRS coordinates

flashcards.definition: Real-time corrections modeled across a network of reference stations; VRS synthesizes a virtual base near the rover.
Continuously Operating Reference Stations — permanent NGS GNSS stations with known coordinates and free data.
NGS Online Positioning User Service; returns NSRS coordinates from an uploaded static file processed against CORS.

lessons.title: Week 10: GPS Survey Planning and Mission Planning
learning_objectives: Plan a GNSS survey using satellite availability and DOP predictions
Set appropriate elevation masks and observation windows
Conduct site reconnaissance for sky visibility and multipath
Choose the right GNSS method for the accuracy required
key_takeaways: Mission planning uses the almanac to predict satellite count and DOP
Avoid windows with high PDOP or few satellites
Site recon identifies obstructions, multipath, and access
Match the method (static/RTK/network) to accuracy and logistics

content.html:
Why plan?

Although modern multi-constellation receivers see many satellites, planning still prevents wasted trips. Mission-planning software uses a current almanac and the site coordinates to predict, for any time window, how many satellites will be visible, where they will be (sky plot), and what the DOP values will be. The surveyor schedules critical occupations for windows with adequate satellites and low PDOP, and avoids the brief windows when geometry is poor.

Elevation mask and obstructions

An elevation mask (commonly 10–15°) tells the receiver to ignore satellites below that angle, because low satellites suffer the most atmospheric delay and multipath. During site reconnaissance, the surveyor evaluates each station’s sky visibility, sketches obstructions (buildings, trees, terrain) on an obstruction diagram, and identifies reflective surfaces that cause multipath. Stations under heavy canopy, beside tall buildings, or near water may need a different method, a longer occupation, or relocation.

content.text: Set a 10-15 degree elevation mask to suppress the noisiest low-elevation signals. On an obstructed site, mission planning helps you pick the window when the visible satellites are well distributed.

content.html:
Choosing the method

Planning includes matching technique to the job. High-accuracy control favors static or OPUS. Wide-area topo favors Network RTK. Tight local stakeout favors single-base RTK. Where real-time links are unreliable, PPK (post-processed kinematic) collects data for later processing. The plan also covers control to occupy, redundancy (repeat occupations on independent windows), equipment, datum/geoid model, and how results will tie to published control.

content.title: Key Takeaways
items: Mission planning uses the almanac to forecast satellite count and DOP
A 10-15 degree elevation mask suppresses noisy low signals
Site recon documents obstructions and multipath risk
Match the GNSS method to the required accuracy and site conditions

flashcards.definition: Using almanac data and site coordinates to predict satellite visibility, sky geometry, and DOP for scheduling observations.
The cutoff angle (commonly 10-15 deg) below which the receiver ignores satellites to reduce atmospheric and multipath error.
A site-recon sketch of azimuth/elevation of obstructions around a station used to assess sky visibility.

lessons.title: Week 11: GPS Data Processing and Network Adjustment
learning_objectives: Describe the GNSS baseline post-processing workflow
Explain loop closures and redundancy checks
Explain least-squares network adjustment of GNSS vectors
Interpret adjustment statistics and error ellipses
key_takeaways: Post-processing computes and quality-checks baseline vectors
Loop closures detect blunders in observed baselines
Least-squares adjustment distributes residuals across a redundant network
Adjustment outputs coordinates with error estimates

content.html:
From raw data to coordinates

Post-processed GNSS work proceeds in stages. First, raw observation files (often in the RINEX exchange format) from each receiver are imported. The software computes each baseline vector by double-differencing and resolving ambiguities, reporting a solution type (fixed is best) and a quality estimate. The baselines, together with their covariances, become the observations for a network adjustment that yields final coordinates on the project datum.

Loop closures and redundancy

Before adjusting, surveyors check loop closures: a closed loop of baselines should sum to zero, so any large misclosure flags a blunder in one of the vectors. Independent baselines (from different sessions or satellite geometry) provide redundancy — the extra observations that let the adjustment detect and absorb small errors. A network with no redundancy cannot be checked; good GNSS networks deliberately observe more baselines than the minimum.

content.text: Redundancy is the surveyor's safety net. Observe more baselines than strictly necessary so loop closures and the least-squares adjustment can catch blunders and quantify accuracy.

content.html:
Least-squares network adjustment

A least-squares adjustment takes all the redundant baseline observations and finds the set of coordinates that best fits them by minimizing the weighted sum of squared residuals. Holding one or more control stations fixed (a constrained adjustment) ties the network to the published datum. The adjustment reports residuals, a reference variance (which should be near 1 if the error model is right), and error ellipses showing the positional uncertainty of each station. These statistics are the surveyor’s evidence that the network meets accuracy standards.

content.title: Key Takeaways
items: Post-processing computes baseline vectors from RINEX data
Loop closures and redundancy catch blunders
Least-squares adjustment yields best-fit coordinates and error estimates
A constrained adjustment ties the network to published control

flashcards.definition: Receiver Independent Exchange format — the standard raw GNSS observation file format for post-processing.
The misclosure of a closed loop of baselines, which should sum to zero; large values flag a blunder.
A method that distributes residuals across a redundant network to find best-fit coordinates and their uncertainties.

lessons.title: Week 12: Introduction to Geographic Information Systems (GIS)
learning_objectives: Define GIS and its components
Distinguish vector and raster data models
Explain layers, attributes, and topology
Relate GIS coordinate systems and projections to GNSS data
key_takeaways: GIS stores, analyzes, and displays spatially referenced data
Vector represents discrete features; raster represents continuous grids
Data are organized in thematic layers with attribute tables
Topology encodes spatial relationships like adjacency and connectivity

content.html:
What a GIS is

A Geographic Information System (GIS) is software (and the associated data, people, and procedures) for capturing, storing, analyzing, and displaying data tied to locations on the Earth. Unlike a static map, a GIS links geometry (where features are) with attributes (what they are) in a database, so you can query, measure, and analyze relationships. Surveyors feed authoritative, high-accuracy field data into GIS and increasingly consume GIS data for parcels, utilities, and base mapping.

Vector and raster

GIS uses two fundamental data models. The vector model represents discrete features as points (a well, a monument), lines (a road, a pipe), and polygons (a parcel, a lake), each with coordinates and an attribute record. The raster model represents continuous phenomena as a grid of cells, each holding a value — aerial imagery, elevation (a DEM), or land cover. Vector is precise and compact for boundaries and networks; raster is natural for surfaces and imagery. Many projects use both.

Layers, attributes, and topology

GIS organizes data into thematic layers (parcels, streets, utilities, contours) that overlay in a common coordinate system. Each vector feature links to a row in an attribute table — the descriptive data (owner, diameter, elevation). Topology is the set of rules encoding spatial relationships independent of exact coordinates: which polygons are adjacent, whether lines connect at nodes, whether parcels overlap or leave gaps. Topology enforces data integrity (no parcel gaps/overlaps) and enables analysis like tracing a network.

content.text: For GIS to be useful, every layer must share a defined coordinate system and projection. Mismatched or undefined projections are the most common reason layers do not line up.

content.title: Key Takeaways
items: GIS links geometry with attributes in a spatial database
Vector = discrete points/lines/polygons; raster = continuous grid cells
Data are organized in layers, each feature having an attribute record
Topology encodes adjacency, connectivity, and integrity rules

flashcards.definition: Vector represents discrete features as points/lines/polygons; raster represents continuous phenomena as a grid of cells.
The database table linking each GIS feature to its descriptive data (owner, size, type).
Rules encoding spatial relationships (adjacency, connectivity) and enforcing integrity such as no gaps or overlaps between polygons.

lessons.title: Week 13: GIS Data Collection, Quality, and Analysis
learning_objectives: Describe GIS data sources and georeferencing
Explain metadata and the components of spatial data quality
Perform basic spatial analysis: buffers, overlay, clip
Relate GNSS positional accuracy to GIS data quality
key_takeaways: GIS data come from GNSS, surveys, imagery, and existing records
Georeferencing ties raster/scanned data to real-world coordinates
Metadata documents source, accuracy, datum, and lineage
Buffer and overlay are core spatial-analysis operations

content.html:
Getting data into GIS

GIS data come from many sources: GNSS field collection (the surveyor’s contribution), conventional surveys, aerial and satellite imagery, LiDAR, scanned historical maps, and existing digital records. Raster and scanned sources must be georeferenced — assigned real-world coordinates — by matching identifiable control points (road intersections, monuments) to known coordinates so the image overlays correctly with other layers. Field GNSS data, by contrast, already carry coordinates and are imported directly.

Data quality and metadata

Not all GIS data are equally trustworthy. Spatial data quality has several components: positional accuracy (how close geometry is to truth), attribute accuracy (are the descriptive values correct), completeness, logical consistency (topology valid), and lineage (the data’s history and processing). Metadata — “data about the data” — documents all of this: source, collection method, datum and projection, accuracy, date, and steps applied. Surveyors providing data to a GIS should deliver complete metadata so downstream users know the datum, epoch, and accuracy.

content.text: A GIS layer is only as reliable as its worst input. Sub-meter mapping-grade GNSS is fine for asset inventory but must never be confused with survey-grade boundary data. Document accuracy in metadata so users do not over-trust the layer.

content.html:
Core spatial analysis

GIS earns its keep through analysis. A buffer creates a zone of a specified distance around features (e.g., a 100-ft wetland setback). Overlay operations combine layers: a union keeps everything, an intersect keeps only the common area, a clip cuts one layer to the outline of another. Spatial queries select features by location (“parcels within 500 ft of the floodplain”). These tools turn raw layers into answers — which is exactly where accurate GNSS-collected geometry pays off.

content.title: Key Takeaways
items: GIS data come from GNSS, surveys, imagery, and records; rasters need georeferencing
Quality = positional + attribute accuracy + completeness + consistency + lineage
Metadata documents source, datum, accuracy, and processing
Buffer and overlay (intersect/union/clip) are core analysis tools

flashcards.definition: Assigning real-world coordinates to a raster/scanned image by matching control points so it overlays other layers.
"Data about the data" — documentation of source, datum, projection, accuracy, date, and lineage.
A spatial-analysis operation creating a zone of specified distance around features.

lessons.title: Week 14: Integrating GNSS Data with CAD and GIS Workflows
learning_objectives: Describe the GNSS-to-CAD-to-GIS data flow
Explain coordinate/datum reconciliation between field and GIS
Apply grid-to-ground conversion using the combined scale factor
Identify file formats and feature coding for integration
key_takeaways: Field GNSS data flow into CAD for design and GIS for management
Datum, projection, and geoid model must agree across systems
Grid and ground distances differ by the combined scale factor
Feature codes and standard formats streamline the handoff

content.html:
The integrated workflow

A surveyor’s GNSS observations rarely live in isolation. The typical flow is: collect points and linework in the field with feature codes, reduce and adjust the data, bring it into CAD for design, plats, and earthwork, and publish it to GIS for long-term asset and parcel management. Each handoff must preserve coordinate system, units, and accuracy. The surveyor is often the source of the most accurate geometry in the entire enterprise GIS, so clean integration matters.

Grid vs. ground distances

The biggest integration pitfall is mixing grid and ground coordinates. State plane coordinates are grid values: a horizontal distance computed from them differs from the distance you would tape on the ground because of two effects. The scale factor accounts for the map projection, and the elevation (sea-level) factor accounts for reducing a distance at elevation down to the ellipsoid. Their product is the combined scale factor (CSF). Ground distance times CSF gives grid distance; grid distance divided by CSF gives ground.

content.label: Combined scale factor: projection scale k times elevation factor

content.text: Texas differs in practice: many Texas projects use surface (ground) coordinate systems or a Texas-specific low-distortion / "surface adjustment factor" so that CAD distances match taped ground distances. Always confirm whether a deliverable is on grid or surface coordinates before combining datasets.

content.html:
Formats and coding

Integration relies on common formats: RINEX for raw GNSS, ASCII point files (PNEZD), LandXML for survey/design exchange, DWG/DXF for CAD, and shapefile/geodatabase/GeoPackage for GIS. Feature coding in the field (assigning each shot a code like EP for edge-of-pavement) lets software draw linework and assign GIS attributes automatically. Consistent datum, projection, geoid model, and units across all these formats is what keeps the data lining up.

content.title: Key Takeaways
items: Field GNSS data flow through CAD into GIS; each step must preserve datum and units
Grid and ground distances differ by the combined scale factor (CSF)
D_grid = D_ground x CSF; divide by CSF to go grid to ground
Texas often uses surface/low-distortion coordinates so ground = CAD distances

flashcards.definition: The product of the projection scale factor and the elevation factor; converts between ground and grid distances.
flashcards.hint_1: Multiply ground by it to get grid

flashcards.definition: Grid is the distance on the projection (state plane); ground is the taped horizontal distance at elevation. They differ by the CSF.
A field-assigned code (e.g., EP for edge of pavement) that drives automatic linework and GIS attribute assignment.

lessons.title: Week 15: GNSS Best Practices and Comprehensive Review
learning_objectives: Summarize GNSS and GIS best practices for accurate survey data
Review field procedures that ensure quality and redundancy
Review the full GNSS-GIS workflow end to end
Prepare for the final exam
key_takeaways: Best practice means redundancy, checks, and documentation
Verify on known control, watch the solution status, and record metadata
The full workflow spans planning, collection, processing, and integration
The final exam covers the entire course

content.html:
Field best practices

Accurate GNSS work is disciplined work. Check into known control at the start and periodically. Record only fixed RTK solutions and watch PDOP and the number of satellites. Build in redundancy — observe key points twice on independent geometry. Measure and record antenna heights carefully (a classic blunder source). Apply the correct datum and geoid model. Document everything: occupation times, equipment, datum, epoch, and accuracy. These habits turn raw fixes into defensible survey data.

GIS and integration best practices

On the GIS side, define and carry a consistent coordinate system on every layer, deliver complete metadata stating datum, projection, geoid model, and accuracy, and never let mapping-grade data masquerade as survey-grade. When moving data between field, CAD, and GIS, confirm grid vs. ground, units, and the combined scale factor. Keeping the lineage documented protects both the surveyor and the downstream users.

rows: Check control, fixed only, redundancy, antenna heights
Consistent datum/units, metadata, grid vs ground

content.text: When in doubt, add redundancy and write it down. A second independent observation and complete metadata resolve most disputes about GNSS data quality.

content.html:
Course review

The course has moved from satellite signals (Weeks 1–2), through errors and reference frames (Weeks 3–5), the major survey methods (Weeks 6–7, 9–11), into GIS and integration (Weeks 12–14). The unifying theme is turning satellite ranges into trustworthy, documented spatial data that flows cleanly into CAD and GIS. Review the four core computations (H = h − N; baseline a + b·D; UERE × DOP; grid/ground CSF) and the key conceptual distinctions for the final.

content.title: Key Takeaways
items: Redundancy, control checks, and documentation are the heart of best practice
Record only fixed solutions; measure antenna heights carefully
Carry a consistent coordinate system and complete metadata into GIS
The final exam covers the entire GNSS-to-GIS workflow

flashcards.definition: Observing key points more than once on independent geometry so blunders can be detected and accuracy quantified.
A common error source; always measure and record antenna height (and measurement type) carefully.
Recording the source, datum, epoch, processing, and accuracy of data so its quality is defensible.

lessons.title: Week 16: Final Exam
learning_objectives: Demonstrate comprehensive mastery of GNSS and GIS applications
Apply all core formulas and concepts from the course
Integrate GNSS field methods with GIS workflows
key_takeaways: The final is comprehensive, covering Weeks 1-15
Both conceptual and computational questions appear
Master the four core formulas and the full workflow

content.html:
Final exam overview

The Week 16 final is comprehensive, spanning the entire course: GNSS fundamentals and constellations, signal structure and observables, error sources and DOP, datums and projections, geoid heights, static and RTK methods, Network RTK/CORS/OPUS, survey and mission planning, data processing and adjustment, GIS fundamentals, GIS data quality and analysis, and GNSS-CAD-GIS integration.

What you should master

Be ready to apply the four core computations: orthometric height H = h − N; static baseline error a + b·D; expected position error UERE × DOP; and grid/ground conversion via the combined scale factor. Be ready to explain code vs. carrier phase and the integer ambiguity, fixed vs. float, WGS84 vs. NAD83, vector vs. raster, topology, georeferencing, metadata, and the role of CORS and OPUS. Scenario questions will ask you to choose the right method and reconcile coordinate systems.

rows: Signals, errors, datums, heights, static, RTK
Network RTK, planning, adjustment, GIS, integration

content.text: Review the four formulas and practice one numeric example of each. Then review the conceptual distinctions; many final questions are scenario-based choices among methods and coordinate systems.

content.title: Key Takeaways
items: The final is comprehensive across Weeks 1-15
Apply all four core formulas confidently
Explain the key conceptual distinctions and choose appropriate methods

flashcards.definition: Comprehensive: all GNSS and GIS topics from Weeks 1-15.
H = h - N; baseline a + b*D; UERE x DOP; grid = ground x CSF.

quiz_questions.question_text: How many satellites are required at minimum for a 3D GNSS position fix?
quiz_questions.explanation: Three satellites solve X, Y, Z; a fourth is needed to solve the receiver clock bias, so four is the minimum.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following is NOT one of the four global GNSS constellations?
quiz_questions.explanation: QZSS is a regional (Asia-Pacific) system. The four global constellations are GPS, GLONASS, Galileo, and BeiDou.

quiz_questions.question_text: The GPS L1 carrier is broadcast at which frequency?
quiz_questions.explanation: L1 is 1575.42 MHz. L2 is 1227.60, L5 is 1176.45, and 10.23 MHz is the fundamental frequency.

quiz_questions.question_text: Survey-grade centimeter accuracy depends primarily on resolving what?
quiz_questions.explanation: Carrier phase is precise but ambiguous by an integer number of wavelengths; resolving that integer gives centimeter accuracy.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which error source can be largely removed by using dual-frequency observations?
quiz_questions.explanation: The ionosphere is dispersive (frequency-dependent), so combining two frequencies removes most of its delay. The troposphere is non-dispersive.

quiz_questions.question_text: If the UERE is 3.0 m and the PDOP is 2.5, what is the expected position error in meters?
quiz_questions.explanation: Position error = UERE x PDOP = 3.0 x 2.5 = 7.5 m.

quiz_questions.question_text: WGS84 and NAD83 coordinates for the same point differ by approximately:
quiz_questions.explanation: The two datums are defined differently and the plates move, so they differ by 1-2 m. They are not interchangeable.

quiz_questions.question_text: The State Plane Coordinate System uses which projection for zones that are wider east-to-west?
quiz_questions.explanation: Lambert conic is used for east-west wide zones; transverse Mercator for north-south tall zones.

quiz_questions.question_text: A GNSS receiver directly measures which type of height?
quiz_questions.explanation: GNSS gives ellipsoidal height h; orthometric H must be derived using H = h - N with a geoid model.

quiz_questions.question_text: A point has ellipsoidal height h = 250.00 m and geoid undulation N = -25.00 m. What is the orthometric height H in meters?
The vector (delta X, Y, Z) between two simultaneously observing receivers is called a:
quiz_questions.explanation: A baseline is the relative 3D vector between two receivers, the fundamental observation of static surveying.

quiz_questions.question_text: A static baseline spec is 5 mm + 1 ppm. For a 10 km baseline, what is the expected error in millimeters?
quiz_questions.explanation: Error = 5 mm + (1 ppm x 10,000 m) = 5 + 10 = 15 mm.

quiz_questions.question_text: In RTK, which solution status is required before recording survey-grade points?
quiz_questions.explanation: Only a fixed solution has resolved integer ambiguities and is accurate to a centimeter or two; float is not trustworthy.

quiz_questions.question_text: Single-base RTK remains reliable at unlimited distance from the base.
quiz_questions.explanation: Single-base RTK degrades beyond roughly 10-20 km as the atmosphere over base and rover diverges.

quiz_questions.question_text: NGS OPUS returns coordinates by processing a submitted static file against:
quiz_questions.explanation: OPUS processes the uploaded dual-frequency static file against three nearby CORS and returns NSRS coordinates.

quiz_questions.question_text: A typical GNSS elevation mask is set to about:
quiz_questions.explanation: A 10-15 degree mask suppresses low-elevation satellites that suffer the most atmospheric delay and multipath.

quiz_questions.question_text: In a closed loop of baselines, the misclosure should ideally be:
quiz_questions.explanation: A closed loop of vectors should sum to zero; a large misclosure flags a blunder in one baseline.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which data model represents continuous phenomena such as elevation or imagery?
quiz_questions.explanation: Raster uses a grid of cells for continuous surfaces (DEM, imagery). Vector represents discrete features.

quiz_questions.question_text: Assigning real-world coordinates to a scanned map by matching control points is called:
quiz_questions.explanation: Georeferencing ties raster/scanned data to real coordinates so it overlays other layers.

quiz_questions.question_text: Name the term for "data about the data" documenting source, datum, accuracy, and lineage.
quiz_questions.explanation: Metadata documents source, datum, projection, accuracy, date, and lineage of a dataset.

quiz_questions.question_text: A ground distance of 1000.00 ft is on a system with a combined scale factor of 0.99990. What is the grid distance in feet?
quiz_questions.explanation: D_grid = D_ground x CSF = 1000.00 x 0.99990 = 999.90 ft.

quiz_questions.question_text: In much of Texas practice, surface (ground) coordinate systems are used so that:
options: CAD/plat distances match taped ground distances
quiz_questions.correct_answer: CAD/plat distances match taped ground distances
quiz_questions.explanation: Texas often uses surface/low-distortion coordinates so that distances in CAD match ground measurements, avoiding grid-to-ground confusion.

problem_templates.name: Orthometric height from ellipsoidal height and geoid undulation
problem_templates.description: Convert a GNSS ellipsoidal height to an orthometric (NAVD88-style) height using the geoid undulation.
problem_templates.question_template: A GNSS observation gives an ellipsoidal height h = {{h:f2}} m at a point where the geoid model reports an undulation N = {{N:f2}} m. What is the orthometric height H (m)?
problem_templates.explanation_template: Orthometric height H = h - N = {{h:f2}} - ({{N:f2}}) = {{_answer}} m. The undulation is negative across the lower 48, so subtracting it adds to the ellipsoidal height.

images_needed.caption: Ellipsoid, geoid, and the relationship H = h - N at a point

problem_templates.name: Static baseline error from constant and ppm terms
problem_templates.description: Compute the expected static baseline error from a fixed (mm) plus length-dependent (ppm) specification.
problem_templates.question_template: A static baseline accuracy is specified as {{a}} mm + {{b}} ppm. For a baseline length of {{km}} km, what is the expected error (mm)?

solution_steps_template.title: Length-dependent term
solution_steps_template.calculation_template: {{b}} ppm x {{km}} km = {{b}} mm/km x {{km}} km

problem_templates.explanation_template: One ppm equals 1 mm per km, so the error is a + b*km = {{a}} + {{b}} x {{km}} = {{_answer}} mm.

problem_templates.name: Position error from UERE and PDOP
problem_templates.description: Compute the expected GNSS position error from the per-satellite range error and the position dilution of precision.
problem_templates.question_template: If the User Equivalent Range Error (UERE) is {{uere:f1}} m and the PDOP is {{pdop:f1}}, what is the expected position error (m)?
problem_templates.explanation_template: Expected position error = UERE x PDOP = {{uere:f1}} x {{pdop:f1}} = {{_answer}} m. Lower PDOP (better geometry) reduces the error.

problem_templates.name: Grid-to-ground distance using the combined scale factor
problem_templates.description: Convert a grid (state plane) distance to a ground distance using the combined scale factor.
problem_templates.question_template: A grid distance of {{grid:f2}} ft is to be reduced to the ground using a combined scale factor CSF = {{csf:f5}}. What is the ground distance (ft)?
problem_templates.explanation_template: Grid distance = ground x CSF, so ground = grid / CSF = {{grid:f2}} / {{csf:f5}} = {{_answer}} ft.

images_needed.caption: Reduction of a distance among ground, ellipsoid, and grid surfaces
